alma

Multimodal AI platform

A conversational interface for text, images, and web search. Built with Flask, React, and the Gemini SDK. The easy path kept breaking, so we built it the hard way.

Open Alma Source

The short version

Alma started as a chat box. Then we added search, thinking, voice, images, conversation history, and a settings panel with range sliders that work differently in Firefox and Chromium. Every feature was earned through debugging, not planning.

We wrote E2E tests. Fifty out of seventy-seven failed on the first run. The Gemini SDK swallowed errors silently. The API returned empty strings and called it success. The dismiss overlay covered the entire viewport. The textarea lost focus after every keystroke.

We fixed it all. Eighty-six out of eighty-six tests pass now.

The easy path

The Gemini SDK has a method called generate_text. It looks simple. It is not.

When the API returns a 429 rate limit error, the SDK raises a ValueError. Our error handler checked if the error was "recoverable" and returned False for ValueError. So the SDK raised the error, our handler caught it, decided it was not recoverable, and returned a generic 500 to the frontend. The user saw "Internal Server Error" with no explanation.

The fix: catch all exceptions except programming errors (TypeError, NameError, AttributeError). Return synthetic text for anything the SDK can recover from. Let real bugs through.

The hard parts

  • E2E tests failed because the disclaimer dialog overlay blocked every click. We targeted .disclaimer-dialog .dialog-btn--confirm to dismiss it.
  • Search tests failed because search_progress was not visible within 5 seconds on slow CI. We added an extended 10-second wait and a dynamic wait_for that checks if the element exists first.
  • Suggestion tests failed because alma_active_conversation in localStorage caused the app to enter "restoring" mode on reload, rendering a different view without suggestions. We clear it before every reload.
  • Slider tests failed because Firefox and Chromium handle range inputs differently. Firefox uses ::-moz-range-thumb and ::-moz-range-track. Chromium uses ::-webkit-slider-thumb. We unified them with a single CSS custom property: --range-progress.
  • Bot reviews from nuntius-review suggested .first.is_visible() instead of is_visible() for Playwright checks. We applied every suggestion.

What works

  • Chat, conversational interface with context across turns.
  • Search, real-time web search with source citations.
  • Thinking, step-by-step reasoning for complex questions.
  • Voice, text-to-speech responses powered by gTTS.
  • Images, paste or drag images directly into the conversation.
  • History, conversations persist locally with search and rename.
